systemsMapSummary: The target behaviour is gated by three reinforcing loops: a friction loop (each extra step at the moment of action compounds drop-off), a salience loop (nothing in the environment cues the behaviour, so it depends on memory), and a social-proof loop (peers only see the old behaviour, so the visible norm pulls the wrong way). The highest-leverage points sit where these loops intersect — early in the journey, before habits set.

topLeveragePoints.point: Remove the highest-friction step at the moment of action
topLeveragePoints.currentState: A required step adds ~2 minutes and a context switch right when motivation peaks.

topLeveragePoints.point: Add a timely environmental cue tied to an existing routine
topLeveragePoints.currentState: There is no reminder anchored to a moment the group already pays attention to.

topLeveragePoints.point: Make the desired behaviour the visible social norm
topLeveragePoints.currentState: Peers only see the old behaviour; the new one is invisible.

topLeveragePoints.point: Confirm completion so people know they 'did it right'
topLeveragePoints.currentState: No confirmation after the first action; users hesitate to repeat.

topLeveragePoints.point: Pre-fill known information to shorten the action
topLeveragePoints.currentState: Users re-enter data the system already holds.

topLeveragePoints.point: Sequence the ask after a moment of demonstrated intent
topLeveragePoints.currentState: The ask lands before intent is expressed, so it reads as noise.

headline: If we changed the moment-of-action friction, the environmental cue, and the visible norm, we'd likely see adoption move from 31% toward the 50% target without new incentives.

behaviors.description: Completes the core action in a single sitting at the decision point
Repeats the action within the following week
Notices a peer performing the new behaviour

keyActors.behavior: Decides whether to complete the action at the decision point
Signals the prevailing norm through visible behaviour
Owns the friction step and the confirmation surface

causeEffect.from: Friction at the moment of action

loops: Friction loop: friction → drop-off → fewer visible adopters → weaker norm → less motivation to push through friction.
Salience loop: no cue → behaviour forgotten → no habit → cue never becomes self-generated.

comb.barrier: The action requires a step many in the group can't complete in the moment.
comb.source: Funnel analytics: drop-off concentrates at the context-switch step

comb.barrier: People are unsure whether they did it 'right', so they hesitate to repeat.
No environmental cue at the decision point; behaviour depends on memory.
comb.source: Behaviour clusters after incidental reminders, then decays

comb.barrier: The desired behaviour is invisible to peers, so the norm signals the opposite.
comb.source: Higher adoption where behaviour is publicly visible (correlational)

comb.barrier: Belief the outcome is worth it is present but fragile under friction.
comb.source: Stated intent high; behaviour falls off only as effort rises

comb.barrier: No habit loop yet; nothing makes the behaviour feel automatic.

strongestBarriers.barrier: Friction at the moment of action blocks capable, motivated users
strongestBarriers.rationale: Highest impact and highly changeable; converts existing intent directly into action.

strongestBarriers.barrier: No environmental cue, so the behaviour depends on memory
strongestBarriers.rationale: High impact; anchoring a cue to an existing routine is a cheap, reversible change.

strongestBarriers.barrier: The new behaviour is invisible, so social proof runs the wrong way
strongestBarriers.rationale: Reinforces the other two levers once the behaviour becomes visible.

barrierNarratives.point: Remove the highest-friction step at the moment of action
barrierNarratives.narrative: Effort at the decision point is the dominant barrier. Because motivation is already high, lowering friction converts existing intent directly into action — the cheapest, fastest lever.

barrierNarratives.point: Add a timely environmental cue tied to an existing routine
barrierNarratives.narrative: Even motivated people forget. Anchoring a cue to an existing routine turns memory-dependence into reliable triggering without adding incentives.

barrierNarratives.point: Make the desired behaviour the visible social norm
barrierNarratives.narrative: Behaviour spreads by imitation. Making the new behaviour visible flips social proof from a headwind into a tailwind, reinforcing the other two levers over time.

gapLog.content: Instrument the week-8 sustained-behaviour measure before launch.
Assign single-team ownership of the friction step to avoid split accountability.
The chosen cue routine actually has the group's attention.
Segment the group by capability vs. motivation barrier to target interventions.

discoveryActivities: 5–8 contextual interviews at the moment of action to confirm the friction step.
A friction audit / journey walkthrough timing each step to the decision point.
A norms survey to measure perceived vs. actual behaviour in the group.
A baseline instrumentation sprint to capture the week-8 sustained metric.
